plugin.name: exponential
plugin.description:
Gives exponential decay d at time t:
d(t)=exp(-t/tr)
where tr is the effective relaxation time.

plugin.name: exponential_rate
plugin.description:
Gives exponential decay rate d' at time t:
d'(t)=-1/tr * exp(-t/tr)
where tr is the effective relaxation time.

plugin.name: netcdf writer
plugin.description:
This data handler writes CrusDe results to
a 4-D netcdf file following the COARDS conventions:

(ftp://ftp.unidata.ucar.edu/pub/netcdf/Conventions/COARDS/coards_conventions).

The four dimensions, and so the dimension names, are:

latitude, longitude, time, direction.

The sematics of the direction indices depend highly on the
order in which the Green's function plug-in allocates the
output fields and in which order the output generating post-
processors are defined. It is tried information to write as
much information as possible about this to the 'history' field.

This plugin creates a netCDF file with either the filename given
in the experiment definition or the default name for output
(experiment.nc) on initialization. At each time step the the
model results are saved to this file for all directions
(x,y,z, other). The file gets deleted during unloading if
no data was written.

Tools such as 'ncview' help to directly examine the result
data. The 'NCO' (netCDF Operators), e.g. 'ncks' help to extract
fields from the result array. The following script snippet for
example extracts values for all latitudes and longitudes at
time=0 and for direction=2 (usually vertical displacement,
refer to respective file history) from the file disk.nc
using NOS's 'kitchen sink'. awk then strips the variable
names from the values so that an ascii table of the format
'x y z' emerges:

ncks -a -P -v crusde_result -d time,0 -d direction,2 disk.nc|
awk '{
split($3, y, "=")
split($4, x, "=")
split($5, z, "=");

if(x[2] != 0 && z[2] != 0)
print x[2],y[2],z[2]
}' > $INPUT

plugin.name: table writer
plugin.description: This data handler writes CrusDe results to an ASCII table file. A header indicates the semantics of the colomns. Data are separated by a whitespace.

plugin.name: elastic halfspace (pinel)
plugin.description:
This Green's function implements the 'elastic halfspace' Earth model case as described by Pinel et al. (2006) for horizontal and vertical displacement:

Gz(r) = g ⁄ π ⋅ (1-ν2) ⁄ E ⋅ 1 ⁄ r



Gr(r) = - g ⁄ 2π ⋅ ( (1+ν)(1-2ν) ⁄ E ⋅ 1 ⁄ r



with Gz(r) and Gr(r) determine vertical and radial displacement at point r (cylindrical coordinates), respectively (filter coefficients).

plugin.name: thick plate (pinel)
plugin.description:
The Earth is modeled as being made of an elastic layer of arbitrary thickness H lying over an inviscid fluid of density ρf. The fully relaxed response of the Earth to load changes is modeled. The fluid is considered to be in equilibrium. We calculate surface displacement for the equation of equilibrium (div(σ)=0) in Lagrangian coordinates.

Boundary conditions:
....

The convention is such that a compression corresponds to a positive stress. At the lower surface of of the plate (at z=H) a pressure equal to the hydrostatic pressure in the fluid and no tangential component are applied. Displacements are calculated by integration of Bessel functions. At the surface (z=0) they are expressed by:

GzH(r) = Gz, elastic(r) ⋅ (1-ν2 g) ⁄ (E π) ⋅ ∫0∞ (B ⁄ D - 1) ⋅ J0(εr) dε



GrH(r) = Gr, elastic(r) ⋅ g ⁄ (2π E (1-2ν)) ⋅ ∫0∞ [ (1-ν2) ⋅ (2ε A ⁄ D + 4ν) ] ⋅ J1(εr) dε



with Gz(r) and Gr(r) determine vertical and radial elastic and GvH(r) and GrH(r) determine vertical and horizontal displacement at point r (cylindrical coordinates), respectively (filter coefficients). Coefficients A,B,D are to be looked up in the original paper, for the expressions are too complex provide them in this poor layout.

plugin.name: final relaxed (pinel)
plugin.description: The final relaxed response is the sum of the thickplate response and instantaneous elastic response.

plugin.name: inverse thick plate (pinel)
plugin.description: The negative thickplate response.

plugin.name: disk load
plugin.description: Implements the simple case of a disk load put on a 2 dimensional area. In case a load history is defined for a simulation it is used to constrain the height for the current time step.

plugin.name: irregular load
plugin.description:
Read load from a file that must be in the following format:

Lon Lat Height


with Lon and Lat being integers in Lambert coordinates and Height determining the load height at point (lon,lat).< In case a load history is defined for a simulation it is used to constrain the height for the current time step.

plugin.name: dirac_impulse
plugin.description: Derivative of Heaviside function or simply the Delta function: f'(t) = delta(t-t0)with f(t) = 0 on t < t0 f(t) = 1 otherwise

plugin.name: boxcar
plugin.description: Boxcar or rectangular or interval function with: f(t) = 1 on t0 <= t <= t1 = 0 otherwise

plugin.name: boxcar_rate
plugin.description: Derivative of boxcar function: f'(t) = delta(t-t0) - delta(t-t1)with f(t) = 1 on t0 <= t <= t1 = 0 otherwise

plugin.name: fourier_series
plugin.description: Provides a Fourier Series for which bias, b_0, of a time series (y-offset), linear trend, b_1, (annual) sine and cosine, s_1, c_1, semi(annual) sine and cosine, s_2, c_2, as well as the number of days (365) in a cycle, l, can be specified: h(t) = b_0 + b_1 * t + c_1 * cos(2*p) + s_1 * sin(2*p) + c_2*cos(4*p) + s_2*sin(4*p) where p = pi* t/l ; with l is period length.

plugin.name: fourier_series_rate
plugin.description: Derivative of fourier_series: Provides a Fourier Series Rate Load history function for whic linear trend, b_1, (annual) sine and cosine, s_1, c_1, semi(annual) sine and cosine, s_2, c_2, as well as the number of days (365) in a cycle, l, can be specified: h'(t) = b_1 - c_1*sin(2*p) + s_1*cos(2*p) - c_2*sin(4*p) + s_2*cos(4*p) where p = pi* t/l ; with l is period length.

plugin.name: heaviside ramp
plugin.description: Heaviside step function with: f(t) = 0 on t < t0 = 1 otherwise

plugin.name: sinusoidal
plugin.description:
Sinusoidal load history that calculates a load height depending on the current time step (t), a period length (p) and the timestep when the load is supposed to be at maximum (dm):

h(t) = hm / 2 [ 1 + cos( 2*π / p * (t-dm) ) ]

with hm being the maximum load height.

plugin.name: sinusoidal_rate
plugin.description: Derivative of sinusoidal.c:Sinusoidal rate load history that calculates a load height depending on thecurrent time step (t), a period length (p) and the timestep when the load is supposed tobe at maximum (dm): h'(t) = Π/p * sin(2Π/p * (t - dm)

plugin.name: fast 2d convolution
plugin.description:
Performs a fast 2D-convolution of load and Green's function based on FFTW3 (http://www.fftw.org).

NOTE: This plug-in will treat only one load function. Use 'fast 3d convolution' if you want to apply multiple load functions.

plugin.name: fast 3d convolution
plugin.description: Performs a fast 2D-convolution of load and Green's function and a 1D convolution of a temporal decay function with a load history based on FFTW3 (http://www.fftw.org)

plugin.name: factor
plugin.description: Multiplies all dimensions with a constant factor to be given in the experiment definition. Useful for e.g. magnitude conversions after processing.

plugin.name: hori2vert-ratio
plugin.description: Calculates the ratio of horizontal and vertical displacement at each point ratio = |Uh/Uz| and adds it to the output in a separate field.

plugin.name: xy2r
plugin.description: Calculates cylindrical coordinate r = √ (x² + y²) and adds it to the output in a separate field.

plugin.name: alma
plugin.description: This plugin implements an interface to ALMA ... still in progress...

plugin.name: elastic halfspace (farrell)
plugin.description: This Green's function implements the 'elastic halfspace' Earth model case as described by Farrell (1972) for horizontal and vertical displacements at depth z.

plugin.name: fourier_series_general
plugin.description: Provides a general Fourier Series with bias, b_0, linear trend, b_1, and up to 5 sine/cosine components each with independent amplitude, period, and phase: h(t) = b_0 + b_1*t + sum_i [ c_i*cos(2pi*t/T_cos_i + phi_cos_i) + s_i*sin(2pi*t/T_sin_i + phi_sin_i) ]
